facts_disclosed: Lost Tanaka Foods, landed Omori Sake brewery

turns.content: man what a couple of weeks. i need to vent but also i think things might actually be turning out okay? i don't even know where to start
Sounds like you've been through it — take your time. What happened? I'm all ears for the vent, and glad to hear there might be a silver lining in there somewhere.

so basically my biggest client dropped me. tanaka foods — they've been like the majority of my income for a while, i did all their packaging. and two weeks ago they just emailed me like 'we're going with an agency going forward, thanks for everything' and that was it. years of work and it's just done in one email

Ouch — losing a major client like that is brutal, especially via email with no real conversation. When someone accounts for a big chunk of your income, it's not just the creative loss, it's an immediate financial hole. And the way they did it — no warning, no chance to discuss or adapt — that stings on a professional level too.

Do you know why they decided to go with an agency? Was it a budget thing, or were they looking for something different creatively?

from what i gathered they want like a full-service agency that can do everything — social media, video content, packaging, the whole marketing stack. which honestly i get, a solo freelancer can't compete with that. but it still sucks because i poured so much into their packaging. like i knew their brand better than their own internal team did

That's the painful irony of being a specialist freelancer embedded in one client's brand — you often understand it more deeply than anyone else, but when they decide they need scale and breadth, that depth doesn't factor into the decision. They're buying convenience, not quality.

And yeah, the full-service agency model is tough to compete against as a solo person. It's not really about whether your work is better (it almost certainly is for packaging specifically), it's about the client wanting one vendor for everything.

You mentioned things might be turning out okay though — what's the other side of this?

okay so this is the part where i feel like the universe was maybe looking out for me a little. literally the same week i was spiraling about losing tanaka, i got contacted by omori sake brewery. they're this craft sake brand that's been getting a lot of buzz lately and they want a complete rebrand — packaging, labels, the whole identity system

The timing on that is almost unreal — that's the kind of thing that makes you believe in freelancer karma. A craft sake brewery wanting a full brand identity is such a different beast from what it sounds like you were doing with the food packaging. How did they find you?

they saw some of my work on my portfolio actually. the person who reached out said they liked that my packaging work felt like it had a story behind it and not just generic product design. which is honestly the nicest thing a potential client has ever said to me lol. and like... the creative brief is incredible. they want something that feels modern but connected to traditional japanese aesthetics. they're not scared of interesting design

That brief alone tells you so much about the client — "modern but connected to traditional Japanese aesthetics" and not being scared of interesting design means they actually have taste and are looking for a designer who can bring vision, not just execute safe templates. That's the dream brief for someone who cares about the craft.

Compare that to what you described with Tanaka — doing solid work but within tight creative constraints. This sounds like it could be the kind of project where you actually get to flex creatively. Sake label design in particular has this incredible tradition of beautiful typography and illustration that you can draw from without it feeling derivative.

Have you started working with them yet, or are you still in the proposal phase?

we had the kickoff meeting last week and i'm already deep into research. like i've been looking at vintage sake labels from the taisho and showa eras and the typography is unbelievable. the way they balanced kanji with decorative elements... honestly i could spend months just studying reference material but i should probably actually start designing at some point lol

The research phase is important though — and for a project like this, understanding the visual history of sake labeling gives you a legitimate foundation to build from rather than just guessing at what "traditional but modern" means. Taisho and Showa era labels are genuinely gorgeous, the hand-lettered kanji on some of those is incredible craftsmanship.

Just set yourself a research cutoff point so you don't disappear into reference gathering forever. Maybe give yourself another week of visual research, then start sketching directions even if they're rough. The research will keep informing the work as you go — you don't need to finish studying before you start making.

What's the scope like? Just the labels, or does the rebrand include things like their website, collateral, tasting room materials?

full identity system — labels for their whole product line, secondary packaging, website, business cards, the works. it's honestly the biggest single project i've ever taken on. which is terrifying but also like... this is exactly the kind of work i got into design to do. not just making things look nice but building an entire visual world for a brand

That's a significant scope — building a complete visual identity system from scratch is the kind of project that can define a chapter of your career. If you nail this, it becomes the centerpiece of your portfolio and the thing that attracts similar clients.

The terror is normal and probably healthy. It means you care about doing it justice. The fact that it excites you creatively rather than feeling like a grind is honestly the most important thing — you'll do your best work when the project genuinely matters to you.

From a practical standpoint, make sure you're scoping the project carefully with clear milestones and revision rounds built in. A project this big with a client this enthusiastic can easily expand if you don't set boundaries early. Are you doing fixed-price or hourly for this one?

fixed price with milestones, which i'm actually proud of myself for negotiating properly for once. i broke it into discovery, concept development, label design, extended identity, and production files. each phase has its own deliverable and payment. i actually learned something from all those years of undercharging lol

Look at you with the milestone-based pricing structure — that's genuinely smart project management. Breaking it into phases protects you from scope creep and means you're getting paid as you go rather than waiting until the entire project is done. If anything changes mid-project, you've got natural check-in points to reassess.

Honestly it sounds like losing Tanaka might end up being one of those things that felt terrible in the moment but pushed you toward better work. Steady safe packaging for a food brand versus building an entire identity system for a craft sake brewery with real creative ambition — that's a significant upgrade in terms of the kind of designer you get to be.

Good luck with it. I'd love to hear how the concept phase goes.

thanks honestly. i was in such a bad headspace two weeks ago and now i'm more excited about work than i've been in like a year. sometimes you need the scary thing to happen to get unstuck i guess. anyway i should get back to my taisho era sake label research lol, talk soon

Enjoy the research — those Taisho labels deserve the deep dive. And yeah, sometimes the thing that feels like a catastrophe turns out to be the door opening. Sounds like you're in a great position to do something really special with Omori. Go make something beautiful.
